passages.quote: And the word is actually made up of two parts, dukkha, which just means a hard place. I don't think anybody would dispute that being a human being on this earth is a hard place.
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the beginning where the base discusses the meaning of dukkha and its translation as 'hard to bear'.
passages.rationale: Provides a memorable, accessible image ('a hard place') that complements the base's explanation of dukkha as 'hard to bear' and makes the concept more relatable.

passages.quote: we have a language such as, you know, I have a big nose, see? I have big ears, I have got big ears, but I am sick, the body is sick, but I am sick, but I have a headache. It's sort of a dualistic relationship that we have to this body.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the section discussing our wrong relationship with the psychophysical organism and identification with the body.
passages.rationale: Offers a concrete linguistic example that vividly illustrates the confused dualistic relationship we have with the body, making the abstract point about identification more tangible.

passages.quote: You don't go to bed making a determination to wake up depressed. You just wake up depressed. And it's these sorts of recognitions that there are things going on inside us, both at the physical and mental level, over which we don't have this control.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the section on anatta where the base discusses lack of control as evidence of not-self.
passages.rationale: Provides a relatable everyday example of lack of control over mental states that powerfully illustrates the anatta teaching about not being in control.

passages.quote: the suffering that we're actually experiencing is all self-created. and that puts you in charge that makes you responsible for your own mental states and what the Buddha is saying is that once you take full responsibility for your mental states for your unsatisfactoriness for your suffering then that's the path out of it
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the base's discussion of how 'Nobody can make me angry, nobody can make me sad' to reinforce the empowering aspect of this teaching.
passages.rationale: Articulates the empowering flip-side of self-created suffering more directly and positively than the base, emphasizing personal responsibility as the path out.

dhammabytes/S09E03_3_The_First_Noble_Tru.title: 3. The First Noble Truth of Suffering

passages.quote: So here, remember, there's not only just the basic suffering, the dukkha, there's the dukkha dukkhata, which means the suffering of sufferings. So he's making always a distinction between the facts of life which are the sickness, old age and death which is painful but the suffering is our relationship to it. So always remember that it's not as though he's denying that there is pain in life. He himself of course never overcame physical pain. He died in pain from some sort of gastroenteritis.
passages.integration_hint: Could be integrated near the base's discussion of dukkha dukkha and the distinction between pain and suffering.
passages.rationale: Adds the concrete example that the Buddha himself died in physical pain, powerfully illustrating that liberation doesn't eliminate physical suffering but transforms our relationship to it.

passages.quote: So I'm sure you've come across this phrase to want what you get more than you're always getting what you want. So wanting what you get moves us towards contentment. This is the way it is. And the more we're content with the way things are, the less there is this desire to want to seek happiness in the pleasurable things of life, just to be contented.
passages.integration_hint: Could enhance the base's discussion of attachment and seeking happiness in the phenomenal world.
passages.rationale: Offers a memorable, pithy formulation ('want what you get') that elegantly captures the shift from craving to contentment.

passages.quote: What is sufficient? And I suggest in this that don't come back on it from greed. Don't look at all the things you greed for and then come back to what you think is sufficiency. Start from the point of view of what you really, really, really need in order to exist. Considering, for instance, the disasters we've had last year like the flooding in Pakistan and the flooding in Australia. So what would you grab and take with you which you think you need? Now that's your basis.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added to the base's discussion of accumulating happinesses and the acquisitiveness that leads to suffering.
passages.rationale: Provides a vivid, practical contemplation exercise using disaster scenarios to help distinguish need from greed.

passages.quote: what was understood by a self at that time okay the self was the center of power of itself so the whole idea of a self was that you were in complete control that's the first thing we'll come back to it the second thing was that it was permanent it wasn't changing it was always the same and the third one was that it was happy it was always happy now if you think about it if you have complete power over your being then you could say to yourself from now on I'm going to be happy and that'd be it that'd be the end of it, wouldn't it? you would be happy all the time and that would presume a state of continuity a state of absolute continuity so this was the idea of a soul, an atta as understood at that time
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing the Brahminical concept of atta/self that the Buddha was responding to, before explaining the process of negation.
passages.rationale: The base mentions the Brahminical concept of self but doesn't clearly articulate the three defining characteristics (control, permanence, happiness) that the Buddha was specifically refuting. This passage provides that precise doctrinal clarification.

passages.quote: nowhere in the scriptures do you see him saying there is no self there's never a metaphysical statement he never goes that far it's a teaching tool he's asking if the self is the center of power and has complete power over what it thinks it is and if it is permanent and if it is always happy then where is it?
passages.integration_hint: Add when explaining anatta as a teaching method rather than a metaphysical claim, near the discussion of 'not this, not that' process.
passages.rationale: This is an important doctrinal clarification that the base lacks - that anatta is a teaching tool/investigation rather than a metaphysical denial. This prevents common misunderstanding of the teaching.

passages.quote: if you think, if we ask ourselves, who am I? Or what am I? One way to look at it is, am I in control of it? Is it completely within my power?
passages.integration_hint: Add to the section on anatta practice, where the base discusses 'no control' as evidence of not-self.
passages.rationale: This offers a simple, practical question for investigation that complements the base's discussion of control but expresses it more directly as a contemplative inquiry.

passages.quote: Are you aware that red blood corpuscles are moving out of the marrow of your bone and into your bloodstream? See, it's a completely weird state we're in.
passages.integration_hint: Insert as a vivid example when discussing lack of control over the body in the anatta section.
passages.rationale: This concrete, visceral example of our lack of awareness and control over bodily processes adds a memorable illustration that the base's discussion of body control lacks.

passages.quote: So it's not change in the way that we would think of change. It's not some sort of continuity. A moment arises, a moment arises, a moment disappears. That's it. There's obviously a connection with the past, but the past has been completely ended for this new moment to begin.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing anicca/impermanence to clarify the radical nature of momentariness versus ordinary change.
passages.rationale: The base discusses impermanence but doesn't explicitly distinguish between ordinary change and the Buddha's radical momentariness. This passage provides that crucial doctrinal clarification.

passages.quote: Continuity gives you expectation. So wherever we believe something to be in any way permanent, even if it's only in a short time, there's always the possibility of suffering.
passages.integration_hint: Add after the discussion of how anicca guides us into seeing dukkha, to explain the psychological mechanism.
passages.rationale: This succinctly explains WHY impermanence leads to suffering through the mechanism of expectation - a connection the base makes but doesn't articulate as clearly.

passages.quote: Which feeling are you? If you say, I am my emotions, I am my emotional life, that's what I really am. I am what I feel. So which one are you going to be? You can't expect an emotion, can you? You don't go to bed and think, well, tomorrow I shall determine to wake up depressed. You wake up depressed. You wake up happy.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the anatta section when discussing disidentification from emotions.
passages.rationale: This provides a vivid, relatable example of lack of control over emotions that complements the base's more abstract discussion of disidentification.

passages.quote: So if you say to yourself, you know, I am a socialist or something, you see, that's it, you're blocked, you can't move. You're frozen into a definition.
passages.integration_hint: Add when discussing how we construct the self through concepts and conditioning.
passages.rationale: This concrete example of ideological identification illustrates how perception/concepts freeze us into false identities - a point the base doesn't illustrate as vividly.

passages.quote: Suppose that this river Ganges was carrying along a great lump of foam and a man with good sight would inspect it, ponder it and carefully investigate it and it would appear to him to be void, hollow, insubstantial. For what substance could there be in a lump of foam?
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the anatta section when discussing how to investigate the body's insubstantiality.
passages.rationale: The base discusses anatta intellectually but lacks the Buddha's vivid imagery. This foam simile for the body adds a memorable, canonical illustration.

passages.quote: Suppose that in the autumn when it is raining and the big raindrops are falling, and a water bubble arises and bursts on the surface of the water. A man with good sight would inspect it, ponder it, and carefully investigate it, and it would appear to him to be void, hollow, and insubstantial. For what substance could there be in a water bubble?
passages.integration_hint: Add alongside the foam simile to illustrate the insubstantiality of feelings.
passages.rationale: The base discusses feelings but lacks concrete imagery. The water bubble simile powerfully conveys the ephemeral nature of vedana.

passages.quote: Suppose that in the last month of the hot season, at high noon, a shimmering mirage appears. Now a man with good sight would inspect it, ponder it, and carefully investigate it, and it would appear to him to be void, hollow, insubstantial. For what substance could there be in a mirage?
passages.integration_hint: Use when discussing how perceptions are constructed by the mind, near the passage about the eye creating pictures.
passages.rationale: The base explains that we create our perceptual world but lacks a simile. The mirage image elegantly captures this illusory quality.

passages.quote: if you think of our emotional thought life, it's layer upon layer isn't it? it's just coming up you're sitting here you know and suddenly off on a dream so if you think of something that makes a little bit more more immediate is an onion you see as you keep peeling the levels of an onion when you get to the middle of it there's nothing there
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the section on disidentifying with thoughts and emotions.
passages.rationale: The base discusses disidentification abstractly. The onion image provides an accessible, contemporary metaphor for the emptiness of mental formations.

passages.quote: when we become disenchanted, the next step is to become dispassionate. It doesn't mean to say that you end up being a sort of emotionally void, a sort of a numb blob. It just means that you don't get passionate about things in the wrong way. Being angry, holding on to things, attachment, you become dispassionate.
passages.integration_hint: Add near the end when discussing how liberation manifests, to clarify what dispassion actually means.
passages.rationale: The base mentions liberation but doesn't clarify what dispassion looks like. This passage prevents the common misunderstanding that Buddhist practice leads to emotional numbness.

foundation_course/S01E03_The_First_Noble.title: The First Noble Truth - The Three Characteristics

passages.quote: This came home very strongly to me when I once went to the optician. My left eye, it seems, had got a little better. I was surprised by it. But the optician told me it was surprising that eyesight remained so static, since the actual cornea, the large lens we look through, changes not once in seven years, nor in a year, nor in a month, but once a week. Yes, indeed, every week I'm looking at the world through a new cornea, and I didn't know it.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing anicca and bodily impermanence, near the section on how we don't truly grasp change despite intellectually knowing it.
passages.rationale: Provides a vivid, personal, and surprising example of radical bodily change that makes the abstract teaching of anicca concrete and memorable.

passages.quote: In other words, it's the difference between an organisation saying it's going to change the staff, meaning training and redeployment, and another organisation which says it's going to change the staff, meaning it sacks everyone it now employs and takes on a totally new workforce. Our bodies change radically.
passages.integration_hint: Use alongside the cornea anecdote to clarify the radical nature of bodily change versus superficial change.
passages.rationale: This analogy powerfully distinguishes between superficial change and radical replacement, clarifying a subtle point about anicca that the base doesn't articulate as clearly.

passages.quote: Nuclear physicists have timed the existence of matter, subatomic particles of which all our bodies are constituted, as a million, million, million, ten thousandths of a second, or one to the power of twenty-three. That's a very small moment of existence indeed. And the Buddha teaches that within that moment of matter existing, seventeen consciousnesses, thought moments, arise.
passages.integration_hint: Add to the section discussing how long a moment lasts and the mind's ability to create continuity.
passages.rationale: Provides precise doctrinal and scientific detail about the speed of arising and passing that enriches the base's more general treatment of momentariness.

passages.quote: We live on this knife edge of time. Awareness, what we are developing in meditation, is a faculty that can only exist in the now. We can't be aware of yesterday. Awareness doesn't live there. We can't be aware of tomorrow. Awareness is not born there. Awareness arises only here and now in this minute moment.
passages.integration_hint: Integrate near the base's discussion of living on the 'knife edge' and the preciousness of the present moment.
passages.rationale: Expresses the same insight about the knife edge of existence more elegantly and connects it directly to the practice of awareness/meditation.

passages.quote: Through meditation, coming to terms with the ever-changing nature of our lives, we free ourselves of false fears and frustrations, fearing the loss of what we cannot actually keep, frustrated by not being able to achieve what is actually unachievable. It leads to a greater realism, and in that greater realism we will find the peace and joy we all so dearly seek.
passages.integration_hint: Could serve as a concluding summary near the end of the chapter where the base discusses hope and movement toward the beautiful.
passages.rationale: Offers a concise, eloquent summary of the practical fruit of understanding the three characteristics that complements the base's conclusion.

passages.quote: So the first thing about not-self is that that's not a philosophical statement. The Buddha's not saying there is no self. It's a teaching tool. It's a way of looking at what we're experiencing and beginning to see that there's no ghost within the machine. There's nothing sort of metaphysical about what we experience. Everything arises because it's in relationship with everything. Everything arises dependent on something. Dependent on something. So nothing has its own essential nature. Nothing has its own being.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when introducing anatta to clarify it's a practical teaching tool, not a metaphysical claim
passages.rationale: The base discusses anatta but doesn't explicitly clarify that it's not a philosophical denial of self - this passage provides an important doctrinal clarification that prevents common misunderstanding

passages.quote: But when I look at this me, if I look at the body, I don't know where the me begins and the me ends. I mean, when does air become me? Does it become me when it's in the mouth or when it's in the blood? When does food I'm eating become me? There's a sort of boundary, isn't there?
passages.integration_hint: Add after discussing disidentification with the body to illustrate the porousness of self-boundaries
passages.rationale: Provides a vivid, concrete illustration of the arbitrary nature of self-boundaries that makes the abstract teaching of anatta more accessible and memorable

passages.quote: So I walk into a supermarket, and there's something like a hundred biscuits there. And I think, fantastic. I have free will to choose any biscuit I want. And I go along the shelf and I come across Crunch It. And I pick it up and it says, Crunch It really puts you in a good mood. And I think, Crunch It? Crunch It? I've never heard of that. I'll put it back. Then you walk back and you see, McVitie's chocolate biscuits. Oh, that's what I want. Chocolate biscuits. I have exercised my free will. Totally conditioned, isn't it?
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the section discussing control and self to illustrate how conditioned our 'choices' really are
passages.rationale: The base mentions control briefly but this humorous, relatable supermarket example makes the point about conditioned choice much more vivid and memorable

passages.quote: In the middle of your sleep, do you say, I'm still here? It's gone, isn't it? There's no me. It's not that there's not awareness there, but there's definitely not a sense of me. If, for instance... The doctor said to you, listen, you've got this very, very strange and terrible disease. If you fall asleep, you will die. Are you going to fall asleep? See? The reason why we fall asleep is because we presume I'm going to appear again in the morning.
passages.integration_hint: Add when discussing consciousness and self-awareness to illustrate the discontinuity of the sense of self
passages.rationale: Offers a striking thought experiment about sleep that illuminates the discontinuous nature of self-awareness in a way the base doesn't address

passages.quote: Are yesterday's feelings the same as today's feelings? Who am I now? Because yesterday I had these feelings, but now I've got different feelings. So who am I? Because the idea of a me is something static, isn't it? Something solid, something I can really point to and say, well, this is me. And the goalposts keep changing.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the section on emotions and identification to emphasize the changing nature of what we identify as 'me'
passages.rationale: Expresses the instability of emotional identity more directly and pointedly than the base, connecting impermanence to not-self in a clear way

passages.quote: And I've noticed that he's now translating delusion as identity. Right. Finally, right. Actually pointing to it. That's the root of it.
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the base's discussion of delusion and anatta, where it explains that delusion means not knowing where you're deluded.
passages.rationale: Provides a precise doctrinal clarification that identity is the root of delusion, sharpening the base's treatment of anatta.

passages.quote: During the pandemic, you'll notice, I'm sure some of you noticed or some of you read about it. Everybody went out and bought loads of toilet paper. What an amazing thing. [...] Why? Well, if you read about what the suppression, the repression of death does to us, you'll notice that we make these connections, right? with shit, excuse the word, with money. That's why money is dirty. Somehow we construe money as being part of death, a part of loss.
passages.integration_hint: Add after the base's discussion of death contemplation and how not bringing death into our lives affects us.
passages.rationale: Offers a vivid contemporary example of how repressed fear of death manifests in irrational behavior, making the teaching more relatable.

passages.quote: So at the end of a work, a piece of work, or you've done a bit of gardening, or you've done this, or you've made a cup of tea, just to stop for a moment and just recognize that's gone, that's finished. That's the end. It's not there anymore. And then just to relax into this present moment, just to stop. And then the next intention comes. And you empower that intention, right?
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the base's discussion of 'Thank you, goodbye' practice and seeing endings of things.
passages.rationale: Provides a practical daily-life method for integrating impermanence contemplation that complements the base's 'goodbye' exercise.

passages.quote: in the zen tradition of korea they have this thing called the wadu which just means what is it what is this what is this and it's just a way of centering that attention constantly asking ourselves right am i seeing this the way it is now [...] It's an attitude. It's an attitude that we're trying to develop of a sort of radical doubt about ourselves as to our ability to see what's actually happening.
passages.integration_hint: Add to the section on investigation and seeing things as they really are, near the discussion of the process of negation.
passages.rationale: Introduces a cross-tradition practice that enriches the methodology for investigating the three characteristics.

passages.quote: And death, in all its aspects of loss, right, every moment is a momentary death. Every time you lose something, you've lost something that was part of you. Even if you only owned it, you didn't describe yourself by it, you didn't identify by it, but it was part of you. It's a little death, a mini death.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the base's section on death contemplation and impermanence, near the discussion of endings.
passages.rationale: Elegantly connects impermanence to death on a moment-to-moment level, deepening the base's treatment of anicca and death contemplation.

dharma_talks/S13E05_An_Attempt_to_Unders.title: An Attempt to Understand the teaching of Not-Self.To play the media you will need to either update your browser to a recent version or update yourFlash plugin

passages.quote: So, how do you describe something which is eternal, doesn't die, is the unconditioned, is not born, not created, and so on? How do you describe something like that? So, eventually, you can only describe it by what it isn't.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when the base discusses the process of negation ('not this, not that') to clarify why the Buddha uses negative definitions for atta/soul.
passages.rationale: This passage provides a clearer doctrinal explanation of why the via negativa approach is necessary when discussing the eternal self/soul, which the base mentions but doesn't fully explain.

passages.quote: If form were soul, this form would not lead to affliction. And it would be possible to determine the body. let my body be thus, let my body not be thus. But because the body is not self, is not the soul, not in charge, this form leads to affliction. This body leads to affliction. And it is not possible to determine our form. So we have minimal control over the body. I can wave my arm around here while I'm speaking and all that, but I can't stop it falling ill, I can't stop it getting older, and I can't stop it dropping dead
passages.integration_hint: Add to the section on anatta where the base discusses control, to provide the scriptural basis and concrete examples of our lack of control over the body.
passages.rationale: The base mentions control briefly but this passage gives the full argument from the Anattalakkhana Sutta with vivid concrete examples that make the teaching more accessible.

passages.quote: When you say, this is mine, what are we actually saying? So when I, like this e-reader you see, Kindle, this is mine. Right? And I'm very attached to it, by the way. Somebody comes along and sees my Kindle and decides they want it. So they take my Kindle. Now I go around saying, somebody stole my Kindle. Somebody's taken my Kindle. It's not mine, is it? It's the thief's. The thief's got it. It's his Kindle. So this, my business, is just a legal fiction.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when the base discusses 'not-me, not-mine' to illustrate how possession is a mental construct rather than reality.
passages.rationale: This contemporary, relatable example of the Kindle makes the abstract teaching about 'mine' concrete and memorable in a way the base lacks.

passages.quote: To kiss a joy as it flies is to live in eternity's sunrise. Blake. But we don't want to do that. We want to catch it. So what is impermanence? Is suffering subject to change?
passages.integration_hint: Add to the section on impermanence and attachment where the base discusses saying goodbye to pleasant experiences.
passages.rationale: The Blake quote elegantly captures the teaching on non-attachment to pleasant experiences that the base discusses more prosaically.

passages.quote: When the Buddha became fully liberated, he did not disappear. Nor did he become a sort of amorphous blob by the side of the road with a sign saying, Liberated. Do not disturb. From other people's point of view, his personality was the same. He looked the same. Visually outside, nothing had happened. But internally, something had changed. And what had changed was that sense of me
passages.integration_hint: Add near the end where the base discusses liberation and the natural arising of wholesome qualities, to clarify what actually changes upon liberation.
passages.rationale: This vivid, humorous image clarifies a common misunderstanding about anatta and liberation that the base doesn't address directly.

dharma_talks/S16E05_The_Body_and_the_Thr.title: The Body and the Three Characteristics.To play the media you will need to either update your browser to a recent version or update yourFlash plugin

passages.quote: if you want to know what it's like to be a body self, just catch the next time you trap your finger in the door. Because just for that one glorious moment, you are the pain. There's just that complete flooding of your consciousness pain. You are the pain. That's the body self.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing identification with the body and the process of disidentification in the anatta section.
passages.rationale: Provides a vivid, relatable example of complete identification with body/sensation that makes the abstract concept of 'body self' immediately graspable.

passages.quote: One of the things about sickness is that the mind produces a future. So as soon as you're sick it's always going to be like this. As soon as you've got something wrong with the body, the mind immediately creates this huge plan where you're going to keep suffering forever. But actually when you come down to the experience of the sickness, just as it is in this present moment, All you have, and I'm talking about just ordinary illness, I mean obviously there are some which are extraordinarily painful, all you have is discomfort and some disability.
passages.integration_hint: Add to the dukkha section where discussing how we create suffering through wrong relationship with experience.
passages.rationale: Offers a practical, psychological insight about how the mind amplifies suffering through projection that complements the base's more theoretical treatment.

passages.quote: One of the things that the self defines itself by is that it's permanent. It never changes. It's always happy. I mean that's how you define a spirit or self or a soul or something. And the whole process of observing impermanence is one of the routes whereby you can liberate yourself.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the anicca section to strengthen the connection between impermanence and not-self.
passages.rationale: Makes explicit the link between permanence as a defining characteristic of self and how observing impermanence undermines self-view—a connection the base implies but doesn't state as clearly.

passages.quote: there's nothing in the world worth holding on to and if you keep repeating that to yourself let it sink in there's nothing in the world worth holding on to worth holding on to nothing in the world it's all that sinks in that there is actually nothing in the world worth holding on to and that makes us very easy with things and it expresses itself through an easy generosity an easy forgiveness you don't hold on to things
passages.integration_hint: Add near the end where discussing how wisdom naturally gives rise to wholesome qualities like generosity.
passages.rationale: Provides a memorable, mantra-like formulation and connects letting go directly to generosity and forgiveness in a way that enriches the base's briefer treatment.

passages.quote: the investigation of the body is constantly undermining that basic identity. And it's understandable it comes to us at birth. Very shortly after birth, at some time in early childhood, there's a growing idea of me as opposed to something else. I mean, they tell us, psychologists tell us that up to about the age of four, we don't seem to have an object. It's just one mass of swimming sensations. And it's all us. There's no distinction between me and somebody else. And out of that looms the first object, it seems, your mother.
passages.integration_hint: Could supplement the Margaret Mahler passage in the base, offering a slightly different framing of the same developmental process.
passages.rationale: Provides an alternative articulation of the child development insight that may resonate differently with readers and reinforces the base's point about identity formation.

dharma_talks/S17E16_Encouragement_Octobe.title: Encouragement October 09.To play the media you will need to either update your browser to a recent version or update yourFlash plugin

passages.quote: by seeing anicca by seeing impermanence you see it's ridiculous to hold on to anything and the Buddha says that's one of his greatest gifts when we see anicca when we see impermanence in its real deepest sense there is nothing absolutely nothing in the world worth holding on to that's the Buddha's word
passages.integration_hint: Insert after the base's discussion of how seeing impermanence leads to non-attachment, to emphasize the Buddha's own statement on this point.
passages.rationale: This adds a direct attribution to the Buddha and a more emphatic formulation of the liberating insight from anicca that the base discusses but doesn't express as forcefully.

passages.quote: this self is the relationship that the knowing has with the world it finds itself in it thinks this is it and as we meditate what we're doing is we're relocating we're relocating out of the body
passages.integration_hint: Add to the anatta section where the base discusses disidentification, to clarify that 'self' is actually a relationship rather than a thing.
passages.rationale: This offers a precise doctrinal clarification that self is a fluid relationship rather than a static entity, which the base implies but doesn't state as clearly.

passages.quote: Instead of saying, I am depressed, we're saying, there is depression. There's some separation, there's some dislocation from it. and that dislocation means that we must be relocating so we must be finding another place within us to observe an emotion
passages.integration_hint: Integrate with the base's discussion of 'there is pain, there is depression' practice to explain the mechanism of relocation.
passages.rationale: While the base mentions the practice of saying 'there is' instead of 'I am', this passage explains the underlying mechanism of relocation more explicitly.

passages.quote: we've relocated three times which we can do by an act of will this is within the power of the self the so called self we've come out of the body we've come out of emotions we've come out of thoughts and images and we've made them objects to observe within us and we've discovered this very peaceful place
passages.integration_hint: Add after the base's discussion of disidentification to provide a clearer map of the three levels of relocation.
passages.rationale: This provides a systematic summary of the three levels of disidentification (body, emotions, thoughts) that the base discusses more diffusely.

passages.quote: there still is the sense of self we are still aware of yourself being the observer so you can't be that either can you? there is another object you see so there is yet one more relocation to go but that becomes completely out of our power because as soon as you try and get rid of a self there has to be another self trying to get rid of it you're into a double bind, you can't go any further
passages.integration_hint: Add to the base's discussion of the observer position being 'also a false position' to explain the double bind more clearly.
passages.rationale: This articulates the paradox of the observer-self more precisely than the base, explaining why we cannot transcend it by an act of will.

passages.quote: So the Buddha doesn't deny that there's a self, he just says it's unreal. And the not-self is a teaching tool. It's a way of saying, well, this is not me, it's not mine, and it's not myself.
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the base's discussion of anatta to clarify the Buddha's position on self.
passages.rationale: This provides a precise doctrinal clarification that the base lacks - distinguishing between denying self versus declaring it unreal, which helps prevent common misunderstandings.

passages.quote: It's a bit like driving a car, isn't it? I mean, when you're in a car driving, you feel you're in control, but actually the car's propelling itself. It's got all sorts of stuff going on and anything can go wrong any time. And then when of course it breaks down or a tire bursts, then you realize you're not in control of the car. The control is quite superficial. So it's the same with the body.
passages.integration_hint: Add after the base's discussion of control and self to illustrate the point about superficial control.
passages.rationale: This vivid car analogy makes the abstract concept of illusory control over the body much more accessible and memorable.

passages.quote: Like with all objects, the ownership of an object is a legal fiction. I mean, we all agree to it. Like, for instance, this is my laptop. But that's just an agreement that we have between us as human beings. The fact is I don't actually own it. And of course, this ownership gets very big because then people own their houses. States own their country.
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the base's discussion of 'not mine' to expand on the nature of ownership.
passages.rationale: This extends the teaching on possession from personal objects to larger social constructs, showing how the delusion scales up.

passages.quote: But even in an arahat, old habits will arise, even those that belong to delusiveness. They'll arise, but they have no power. They're called vasana, they're called leftovers, traces, traces of past habits.
passages.integration_hint: Add to the base's discussion of liberation and the arising of wisdom to clarify what remains after awakening.
passages.rationale: This provides important doctrinal nuance about what liberation actually means regarding habits, which the base doesn't address.

passages.quote: Consciousness only arises when it is impinged upon, when something touches it. So it's like a screen, just like this screen that I'm looking at, like a monitor. There has to be an object. There has to be an object that impinges upon a sense base.
passages.integration_hint: Add to the base's discussion of consciousness and awareness to clarify how consciousness functions.
passages.rationale: The monitor/screen metaphor provides a clear, contemporary image for understanding consciousness that complements the base's more abstract treatment.

passages.quote: Whatever role we're playing in life, father, daughter, gardener, we want to do it well, which is a good motive, no problem there. It's when we over-identify, can't see beyond our role that dukkha suffering emerges. On a good day, we get a buzz out of doing things well, cooking a meal, mending a puncture, teaching a class. But we all have days when these go wrong. The sponge cake turns out as a stodgy lump. The tyre deflates the minute we put the wheel back on. We go blank when trying to teach. This is when the Duke of Identification is felt. The more we identify as a good cook, a good DIYer, a good teacher, the harder it is to bear the letdown.
passages.integration_hint: Could be inserted in the section on dukkha and wrong relationship with the psychophysical organism, to illustrate how identification with roles causes suffering.
passages.rationale: Provides concrete, relatable everyday examples of how identification leads to suffering, making the abstract teaching more accessible.

passages.quote: It's wonderful to have nothing to defend.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added after the discussion of disidentification and the anatta practice, as a pithy summary of its benefit.
passages.rationale: A memorable, elegant one-liner that captures the liberating quality of not-self practice more succinctly than the base expresses it.

passages.quote: Internally, I was experiencing the selfing process, which was generating a confident, delighted sense of me, one who could fix all the printers and gas cylinders in the world. However, the external perspective knew this as delusion. Undermined by that news, the confident self suddenly collapsed into a timid, scared, stiff entity which felt it had no rights to exist. A moment later, the confident self reappeared, wildly insisting it was totally in control of the situation. This yo-yo between elated confidence and abject fear continued for some time.
passages.integration_hint: Could follow the base's discussion of catching how we create suffering and the process of disidentification, as a vivid first-person account of watching selfing in action.
passages.rationale: Offers a detailed, experiential account of observing the selfing process that complements the base's more theoretical explanation.

passages.quote: We start to see how various mes arise and fade depending on external stimuli. When things go well, a happy, pleased, self-confident me emerges. But that fades quickly when things go wrong and the deflated, miserable me takes its place instead.
passages.integration_hint: Could be integrated into the section on anatta and disidentification to show the impermanent, conditional nature of self-identities.
passages.rationale: Bridges anicca and anatta teachings by showing how multiple 'selves' arise and pass away, reinforcing the base's point about no substantial self.

passages.quote: In Vipassana, we can't interfere, we can't tell ourselves to stop being deluded. We have to bear patiently with our greeds, hatreds and delusions. Trust them to burn out in the light of awareness. Teach us the Dhamma as we suffer their flames.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added to the section on meditation practice and catching reactions, as guidance on the proper attitude toward difficult mind states.
passages.rationale: Provides a clear, poetic instruction on the non-interfering quality of vipassana practice that the base touches on but doesn't articulate as directly.

international_talks/S02E01_La_conscience.title: La conscience juste, les obstacles comme chemin vers la libération et les trois caractéristiques.

passages.quote: So, when a child, and sometimes us, when a child sees something that they don't know, they stop dead and they look at it intently. And they have their mouth open, which makes them look a bit stupid. So, the good parent immediately says, stop that, wake up, and they prevent them from waking up at that moment.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added when discussing how we lose direct perception through conceptualization, near the section on delusion and wrong views.
passages.rationale: This vivid example of a child's pure awareness being interrupted illustrates how we lose direct perception - a concrete image the base lacks when discussing delusion.

passages.quote: We are very quick to see something new. But we don't like to see the end of things. Before the end of something, we are already thinking about the next thing, the next thing, the next film to watch, the next person to talk to. The next thing to see. We are always a little bit ahead of ourselves. The reason why we do that is that all endings are a death. A little death, but a death.
passages.integration_hint: Add to the section on impermanence and seeing endings, where the base discusses 'constantly saying goodbye' and putting accent on endings.
passages.rationale: This passage articulates why we avoid endings more eloquently and psychologically than the base, adding depth to the impermanence discussion.

passages.quote: Nothing is lost in this process. Everything is transformed. One was selfish, one finds oneself becoming generous. One was full of hate and anger, one finds oneself being... tolerant, loving. There, one was cruel and malevolent, one finds oneself being more compassionate, more benevolent. It's not something we have to do, it's something that happens quite naturally. The body heals in awareness.
passages.integration_hint: Could strengthen the base's concluding point about how 'the opposite arises quite naturally' when we cut through delusion.
passages.rationale: This passage expresses the natural transformation through awareness more vividly with the healing metaphor, complementing the base's briefer treatment.

passages.quote: So, when something manifests that attracts us, it's like... And everything that is in the balloon wants to escape through this little hole. And if your concentration is good, it's like you are compressing the balloon. So, everything comes out even faster with a lot of force.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added to the section on attachment and craving (tanha) to illustrate how desire operates.
passages.rationale: The balloon metaphor provides a memorable physical image for how desire and attachment work, which the base lacks when discussing tanha.

passages.quote: And the simplest way, when we talk about the body, we say I have a headache, but I am sick. And that is a possession and an identification with this organism. And it's then that we can begin to recognize we only have minimal control.
passages.integration_hint: Add to the anatta section where the base discusses 'no control' as evidence of not-self.
passages.rationale: This concise linguistic example (I have vs I am) provides a practical, everyday illustration of identification that the base's more abstract treatment could benefit from.

international_talks/S07E01_Une_tentative.title: Une tentative de comprendre le non-soi.

passages.quote: Tyto tři způsoby jsou třemi charakteristikami existence. První je jednou, že nahlednutí toho, že věci jaksi vystávají a odcházejí. Druhou věcí je, že vše, když to vystává a odchází, nemohu být, že to nemohu být já. A z psychologického pohledu je to vlastně nahlednutí toho, jak vytváříme utrpení naší mysli. Tím, že máme chybný vztah k věcem, které vystávají a odchází.
passages.integration_hint: Could be integrated near the beginning where the base introduces the three characteristics, offering a concise summary of how they interconnect.
passages.rationale: Provides a clear, succinct formulation of how the three characteristics relate to each other and to the creation of suffering through wrong relationship.

passages.quote: Předtím bychom si mohli říct, já jsem zlobený, já jsem úzkostný. Když si tady sedím, říkám, tam je zlobení, tam je úzkostný. A když sedíme, tak můžeme říct, tak tamhle je zloba, tamhle je úzkost. Tam je radost, tam je láska. Ne já, ne ne. Předtím jsem mohl říct, moje koleno mě bolí. Ale v mojej meditaci teď vidím... A meditaci mohu říct, tak tam je bolest v tom kolení. Ne já.
passages.integration_hint: Insert where the base discusses disidentification practice with 'there is pain, there is unhappiness' - this provides concrete examples of the shift.
passages.rationale: Offers vivid practical examples of the linguistic shift from 'I am' to 'there is' that supports the anatta teaching in meditation practice.

passages.quote: Nás zajímá náš vztah k bolestí, protože chceme vědět, jestli je bolest utrpením, ale nebude to můj vztah, mé vztahování k bolestí, které je utrpením.
passages.integration_hint: Could strengthen the section on dukkha where the base discusses our relationship to unpleasant experiences.
passages.rationale: Precisely articulates the distinction between pain itself and suffering as arising from our relationship to pain - a key doctrinal point about dukkha.

passages.quote: Jakmile mám tu jasnou objektivitu, Můj inteligence je osvobozená od zaslepenosti. A proto se můj inteligence může vidět více jasně, A proto může vidět jasný to, co zaklouším.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added to the section on disidentification where the base explains how stepping back allows wisdom to arise.
passages.rationale: Explains the mechanism by which disidentification leads to clearer seeing - intelligence freed from blindness can see more clearly.

passages.quote: So when Sariputta is asked, you talk about the bliss, the happiness of emotions, but there are no emotions in Nibbana. He says, yes, he says. It's the very absence of emotions which is the bliss in Nibbana. So there's a real conundrum for us.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added when discussing the ultimate goal of practice and what liberation actually feels like, perhaps near the end when discussing the 'beautiful' arising naturally.
passages.rationale: This Sariputta reference adds a precise doctrinal clarification about the nature of Nibbanic bliss that the base lacks, addressing a common misconception about enlightenment.

passages.quote: Nobody can cause us psychological pain. It's all been caused inwardly by ourselves. It's really difficult, if you're new to this, to really sort of grasp that. And don't worry about it if you disagree. Nobody can cause a psychological pain. Now, the disappointment in that is that you can no longer blame somebody else. This is a real disappointment, because up until now it was great. Society, my parents, the dog, they were all to blame. But now, it's just you. But the liberation is that if I cause this, if I cause these awful mental states that I have to bear, I must be able to undo them.
passages.integration_hint: Insert where the base discusses 'Nobody can make me angry, nobody can make me sad' to expand on the psychological implications and the liberating flip-side.
passages.rationale: This passage expresses the same point as the base but with more punch and humor ('Society, my parents, the dog'), and crucially adds the liberating consequence that the base doesn't fully articulate.

passages.quote: So when somebody has annoyed us, when somebody has annoyed us, they've not caused the anger. It's really important. What's happened is we ourselves have come from a position of this. This person has disturbed this position and we've reacted from inside towards them with anger. The anger is caused by us. So therefore, seeking happiness by destroying the other is not going to help. You can annihilate everybody else in the world and you'll still end up angry and lonely.
passages.integration_hint: Could strengthen the section on aversion and the psychology of suffering, near where the base discusses 'acquisitiveness, aversion and fear.'
passages.rationale: The vivid image of annihilating everyone and still being 'angry and lonely' is a memorable way to illustrate the futility of external blame that the base doesn't capture.

passages.quote: So every time we move into the position of the observer within ourselves, we're transcending the body. We're transcending emotion. We're transcending thought. We're finding a very different position within ourselves.
passages.integration_hint: Add near the discussion of relocating to the observer position, where the base talks about 'finding this observation post within ourselves.'
passages.rationale: This succinctly frames the observer position as 'transcendence' and connects it to the Buddha's title Tathagata, adding doctrinal depth the base lacks.

passages.quote: When you get it all together and it's all working properly, we call that human being. But when you take it apart, what is there? But it's just a combination of objects, which the Buddha calls aggregates.
passages.integration_hint: Could be inserted in the anatta section where the base discusses the psychophysical organism and disidentification.
passages.rationale: This offers a clear, accessible explanation of the aggregates teaching that complements the base's discussion of not-self without duplicating it.

youtube_channel/S01E18_14_Not-self_Revis.title: 14 Not-self Revisited The story of Sati MN38

passages.quote: What do you identify with? Now we have all sorts of identities. You know, I'm a father, a son, husband, wife, brother, daughter, workmate, et cetera, et cetera. I also have a social relationships, which I identify with my job. You know, I am a, I'm a worker, I'm an office worker, etc., etc. But what we're talking about is your existential identity. What are you in the face of death? That's where the answer, that's what we're trying to discover or find out.
passages.integration_hint: Could be integrated in the anatta section where the base discusses disidentification, to clarify what kind of identity Buddhism is addressing.
passages.rationale: This passage provides a clear distinction between social/conventional identities and existential identity that the base doesn't explicitly make, helping readers understand the specific target of the anatta teaching.

passages.quote: I was with a friend down in Cornwall. He's died, unfortunately. I used to go down there and spend some time with him. And he was living with his mother and father. [...] And we were having a discussion about something. And his father was seemingly listening to what was on the television. Anyway, at some point in our supposedly private, quiet discussion, I said to him, well, we're not the body. And his father suddenly burst into this declaration, yes, we are, you see. And it was such a loud snap that silence and embarrassment descended upon the four of us for a little while before we continued our discussion. So ironically, of course, he died that very year.
passages.integration_hint: Could replace or complement the similar Cornwall anecdote in the base, as this version provides more context and the poignant detail about the father dying that year.
passages.rationale: The base has a briefer version of this story. This fuller version adds emotional depth and the ironic timing of the father's death, making the teaching about body-identification more impactful.

passages.quote: consciousness at the point of contact, it's just a screen upon which the object the sense base work to create something on a screen that's what that's all it is a screen as something that allows something to appear
passages.integration_hint: Could be added to the base's discussion of consciousness and the phenomenal world being created by the mind.
passages.rationale: This 'screen' metaphor offers a vivid, accessible image for understanding consciousness that complements the base's more abstract discussion of how the mind creates the phenomenal world.

passages.quote: that is, this speculative view is called a thicket of views, a wilderness of views, a contortion of views, a vacillation of views, the fetter of views. So fettered by the fetter of views, the untaught ordinary person is not freed from birth, aging and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief and despair. He is not free from suffering, I say.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added to the base's discussion of delusion and wrong views about self to show the Buddha's own vivid language about such views.
passages.rationale: This direct quote from the suttas provides authoritative scriptural support and memorable imagery ('thicket of views, wilderness of views') that the base lacks when discussing wrong views about self.

passages.quote: I mean, the one, the one example I use, which I know some of you know, is about ice cream, you see. So, as soon as you see ice cream, then of course, There's the memory of it. And there's the, there's that sense of like, so that's the Veda like it. So seeing it, it has to go through that process of recognizing it as ice cream. And then there's the liking of it. Then there's the one of it. And then there's the, I want, and as soon as the I comes in, it's very difficult to stop the empowerment and that's the getting right. So even though I say I like, want, get ice cream, actually psychologically, in Buddhist understanding, it's like, want, I, get, I scream. That's the process. And it's breaking that up that you realize that this identity is necessary, right? It's just something we put into that process.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the section discussing how the sense of self is constructed and how identity arises in the perceptual process.
passages.rationale: This ice cream example provides a concrete, memorable illustration of how the 'I' is actually the last step in perception, not the first—a point the base touches on but doesn't illustrate as vividly.

passages.quote: now remember Narcissus looks into the pool this very beautiful young man and he falls in love with himself with the image and he goes to embrace himself and of course falls in and drowns now in mythology water is creation normally it's often used for an image for creation and Narcissus is sitting on the bank And he looks onto the surface of the water, into the water, and he catches his own image. Now, that's exactly what's happening with this satipanya, with this intuitive awareness, with the Buddha within. It looks into the mind, the heart-mind complex, the body-heart-mind complex, the psycho-physical organism. And what it sees is that surface consciousness upon which everything is rising, and it captures its own image. And that's the sense of the observer, the feeler, the experiencer, the sense of presence.
passages.integration_hint: Add near the base's discussion of the observer/witness position and the mirror analogy already present.
passages.rationale: The Narcissus myth provides a powerful metaphorical framework that complements and deepens the base's mirror analogy, explaining how awareness captures its own image and how we 'drown' in delusion.

passages.quote: We know what's inside the body, the feelings, the sensations. We know what's inside the heart, the emotions, the moods. And we know what's inside the mind, thoughts, images. What's inside the observer? And that's one way of sort of indicating that it's something which is of a completely different order. It doesn't belong to this psychophysical organism.
passages.integration_hint: Insert after the base's discussion of turning the gaze inward toward the knowing and asking what constitutes it.
passages.rationale: This passage offers a clear, practical contemplation question that complements the base's instruction to investigate what the knowing is made of, providing a structured approach.

passages.quote: So remember, the Buddha is not saying there's no self. He's saying that any self is manufactured. It's being constructed. And our whole purpose is to deconstruct that.
passages.integration_hint: Use to clarify the anatta teaching in the section where the base discusses not-self and disidentification.
passages.rationale: This is a more precise and elegant formulation of the anatta teaching than what appears in the base, clarifying the common misunderstanding that Buddhism teaches 'no self' rather than 'not-self.'

passages.quote: Whatever role we're playing in life, father, daughter, gardener, we want to do it well, which is a good motive, no problem there. It's when we over-identify, can't see beyond our role, that duca suffering emerges. On a good day, we get a buzz out of doing things well, cooking a meal, mending a puncture, teaching a class. But we all have days when these go wrong. The sponge cake turns out as a stodgy lump. The tire deflates the minute we put the wheel back on. We go blank when trying to teach. This is when the Duke of Identification is felt. The more we identify as a good cook, a good DIYer, a good teacher, the harder it is to bear the letdown.
passages.integration_hint: Could be inserted in the section discussing how delusion manifests through wrong relationship with the psychophysical organism, to illustrate everyday identification with roles.
passages.rationale: Provides concrete, relatable examples of how identification with roles creates suffering - a practical illustration the base lacks when discussing the abstract concept of wrong self-identification.

passages.quote: It's wonderful to have nothing to defend.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added near the end of the base where it discusses how letting go of attachment undermines the sense of isolated self.
passages.rationale: A memorable, pithy expression of the benefit of non-identification that captures the liberation teaching elegantly in one sentence.

passages.quote: Emotional storms have so much energy that negative thoughts and feelings we normally suppress come skyrocketing into consciousness. In the light of awareness, their pain becomes so acute that our system is forced to learn a new way of coping with the dukkha. This new coping mechanism learned from within, from moment to moment awareness, opens up new doorways in the mind.
passages.integration_hint: Could enrich the section on working with unpleasant experiences and the practice of not identifying with emotions.
passages.rationale: Offers a positive reframe of difficult emotional experiences as opportunities for insight - adds a dimension the base doesn't explicitly address about the transformative potential of emotional difficulty.

passages.quote: What is happening is that unskilful desire, tanha, has driven us to create a new identity so as to fulfil this desire. The better we get to know this experience of selfing, the more we are persuaded of the Buddhist teaching on anatta, not self. Knowing from our direct experience that a me has suddenly arisen makes it hard to take me too seriously.
passages.integration_hint: Could be integrated into the section explaining tanha and its relationship to self-creation, after the base discusses how attachment manifests.
passages.rationale: Introduces the useful term 'selfing' and clarifies the mechanism by which tanha creates identity - a doctrinal precision the base approaches but doesn't articulate as clearly.

passages.quote: I became grateful for the fact that the body can repel the mind as well as attract it.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added to the section on disidentification practice, where the base discusses using 'there is' language to push experience away from self.
passages.rationale: Offers a fresh insight about how unpleasant bodily sensations can serve the disidentification process - a practical observation that complements the base's teaching on working with the body.

passages.quote: So the Buddha doesn't deny that there's a self. He just says it's unreal. And the not self is a teaching tool. It's a way of saying, well, this is not me, it's not mine, and it's not myself.
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the beginning of the anatta discussion to clarify the common misconception about self-denial.
passages.rationale: The base discusses anatta but doesn't explicitly address the common confusion that Buddha denies self exists. This passage provides a clear doctrinal clarification.

passages.quote: It's a bit like driving a car, isn't it? I mean, when you're in a car driving, you feel you're in control, but actually the car's propelling itself. It's got all sorts of stuff going on and anything can go wrong any time. And then when, of course, it breaks down or a tire bursts, then you realize you're not in control of the car. The control is quite superficial. So it's the same with the body.
passages.integration_hint: Add after the base's discussion of control over the body ('If I am my body, I should be able to say, right, from now on you're never going to get ill').
passages.rationale: This car analogy vividly illustrates the superficiality of bodily control in a relatable, everyday way that the base lacks.

passages.quote: Like with all objects, the ownership of an object is a legal fiction. I mean, we all agree to it. For instance, this is my laptop. But that's just an agreement that we have between us as human beings. The fact is, I don't actually own it.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the section discussing 'not mine' to illustrate how ownership itself is a convention.
passages.rationale: The base discusses 'not mine' but doesn't explain ownership as a social fiction. This adds philosophical depth to the teaching.

passages.quote: Now when you're meditating, you're taking a position within yourself of identifying with the observer, the feeler. Once you're in that position, everything's an object, and it's not under your control. The body offers feelings, sensations, the heart offers its moods and emotions, and the mind its thoughts and images, whether you want it to or not.
passages.integration_hint: Add to the meditation instruction section where the base discusses 'finding this observation post within ourselves'.
passages.rationale: This passage more concisely expresses how the observer position reveals lack of control, complementing the base's longer explanation.

passages.quote: This was the big breakthrough of the Buddha, this problem of identity. If identity manifested as delusive action, it caused dukkha. So that's why the Four Noble Truths are phrased in what we can actually experience, unsatisfactoriness, suffering. But the root cause is this problem of identity.
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the opening discussion connecting Four Noble Truths to the three characteristics.
passages.rationale: This explicitly links identity as the root cause to dukkha, providing a clearer doctrinal framework than the base offers.

passages.quote: And I've noticed that he's now translating delusion as identity. Finally, actually pointing to it. That's the root of it. That's the whole, that discourse that the Buddha gives, his second discourse, which allows the five companions of his to become fully liberated. Now, that's not magic. Remember, they'd been practicing like him for years with these absorption states and with the more self-mortification things about not eating and all that sort of stuff and they all become liberated after this talk and what's the talk on is the talk about not self and what that talk is simply saying is is the idea of me as a person is that real can I substantiate it
passages.integration_hint: Could strengthen the section on anatta where the base discusses disidentification and the deeper level of not-self.
passages.rationale: Provides a doctrinal clarification linking delusion directly to identity, and contextualizes the second discourse on not-self as the key to liberation.

passages.quote: during the pandemic you'll notice I'm sure some of you noticed or some of you read about it here everybody went out and bought loads of toilet paper what an amazing thing [...] if you read about what the suppression the repression of death does to us you'll notice that we make these connections right with if you excuse the word with money that's why money is dirty somehow we construe money as being part of death about of loss and so this not bringing that death into our lives right is really undermining us it's not opening us up it's making us clamp down with fear
passages.integration_hint: Could be inserted in the section on death contemplation to illustrate how repression of death manifests in modern behavior.
passages.rationale: Offers a vivid contemporary example of how fear of death operates unconsciously, making the teaching on death contemplation more relatable.

passages.quote: so that in the morning if you were to say to yourself I might not live the whole of this day. I might actually die, or I might see the first signs of a terminal illness. Now at first you might think, well, if I do that, I'm just gonna get depressed. But actually the opposite happens by bringing death as a possibility in our lives at all times, right? [...] the effect is to make us realize how amazing life is how we have this consciousness in the first place how is it there is something anyway
passages.integration_hint: Could enrich the base's discussion of death contemplation where it mentions this practice might seem lugubrious but actually puts life into balance.
passages.rationale: Expresses more eloquently the paradoxical life-affirming effect of death contemplation, complementing the base's briefer treatment.

passages.quote: in the zen tradition of korea they have this thing called the wadu which just means what is it what is this what is this and it's just a way of centering that attention constantly asking ourselves right am I seeing this the way it is now when I say constantly asking ourselves I'm not saying that we should be there asking ourselves constantly what is this [...] it's an attitude it's an attitude that we're trying to develop of a sort of radical doubt about ourselves as to our ability to see what's actually happening
passages.integration_hint: Could be added to the section on investigation and seeing things as they really are, near the discussion of the process of negation.
passages.rationale: Adds a practical contemplative technique from another tradition that illuminates the investigative attitude needed for seeing the three characteristics.

passages.quote: Even in relationships, you know, to try and hold a relationship as it is, you know, you're not the person I began to live with, you're not the person I married and all that. Those sorts of statements don't make any sense within a situation which is in a state of continuous change.
passages.integration_hint: Could be inserted in the anicca section where the base discusses forming a different relationship to the world through seeing impermanence.
passages.rationale: Provides a relatable everyday example of how attachment to permanence causes suffering in relationships.

bhante_essays/S03E03_The_First_Noble_Tru.title: The First Noble Truth of Suffering -- The Three Characteristics

passages.quote: This came home very strongly to me when I once went to an optician. My left eye, it seems, had got a little better. I was surprised by it, but the optician told me it was surprising that eyesight remained so static since the actual cornea, the large lens we look through, changes not once in seven years, nor in a year or in a month, but once in a week! Yes indeed! Every week I'm looking at the world through a new cornea. And I didnt know it!
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing impermanence at the physical level, near the discussion of how things change radically.
passages.rationale: Provides a vivid, personal, and surprising scientific example of radical bodily change that makes the abstract teaching of anicca concrete and memorable.

passages.quote: The Buddha I'm sure would have pointed out that no one steps into the same river with the same foot for that too is ever changing. We can't say this is my body because as soon as we say this is body and thereby identify with it, define the self by it, it's gone, it's changed. It is like trying to grasp water. It just flows out of the hand.
passages.integration_hint: Add to the section on impermanence leading to anatta, where the base discusses how anicca takes away substantiality.
passages.rationale: Extends the classic Heraclitus river metaphor with a Buddhist twist and adds the evocative image of grasping water, expressing the point more elegantly.

passages.quote: We live on this knife-edge of time. Awareness, what we are developing in meditation, is a faculty that can only exist in the now. We can't be aware of yesterday. Awareness does not live there. We can't be aware of tomorrow. Awareness is not born there. Awareness arises only here and now in this minute moment.
passages.integration_hint: Place near the base's discussion of 'living with insecurity' and being 'one heartbeat away from death' to deepen the teaching on present-moment awareness.
passages.rationale: Offers a precise doctrinal clarification connecting impermanence, time, and the nature of awareness that the base touches on but doesn't articulate as clearly.

passages.quote: If I define myself in my depression - what am I when happiness arises? If I say I am all my moods and emotions, then I fall into the error of believing is existing yesterday when I was depressed, now when I am angry and tomorrow when I will be happy! But this is only now, and this now passes away. It is delusion to identify with the past and the future.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the anatta section where the base discusses disidentifying with emotions ('there is depression, there is anxiety').
passages.rationale: Provides a clear logical argument for why identifying with changing mental states is delusional, complementing the base's practical instructions with doctrinal reasoning.

passages.quote: Through meditation, coming to terms with the ever-changing nature of our lives, we free ourselves of false fears and frustrations, fearing the loss of what we cannot actually keep, frustrated by not being able to achieve what is actually unachievable. It leads to a greater realism and in that greater realism we will find the peace and joy we all so dearly seek.
passages.integration_hint: Could serve as a closing summary or be placed near the base's conclusion about movement 'towards the beautiful.'
passages.rationale: Expresses the liberating fruit of understanding the three characteristics more eloquently and succinctly than the base's conclusion.

tips_of_the_day/S05E17_Why_are_we_asked_.title: Why are we asked to observe - impermanence.

passages.quote: As this gathers in focus, time collapses into minute moments of arising and passing away. We are not seeing the world out there arising and passing away, but individual acts of cognition. The amazing thing is that when a moment of cognition comes to an end, still there is awareness.
passages.integration_hint: Insert after the base's discussion of how the mind creates continuity and how we catch 'the insecurity of our lives' in each moment.
passages.rationale: This adds a crucial clarification missing from the base: that in deep practice we observe cognition itself arising and passing, not external objects, and that awareness persists beyond each cognitive moment—pointing toward the deathless.

passages.quote: This is how we come to realise that we are not acts of cognition, which include all the khanda. The Aggregates of our physicality, perceptions, feelings, mental states and acts of cognition are not me, not mine, not a substantial self.
passages.integration_hint: Add near the base's discussion of disidentification and the five aggregates, where it describes separating 'what constitutes the person.'
passages.rationale: Provides a concise doctrinal statement linking the khandas explicitly to acts of cognition and not-self, reinforcing the base's teaching on disidentification with clearer terminology.

passages.quote: The corollary is to experience what we are – that awareness. Awareness is the deathless. This is momentary dropping of the fetters of ignorance and delusive desire. A moment, like a flash of lightening into Nibbana.
passages.integration_hint: Insert after the base's discussion of turning the gaze inward toward 'the knowing' and asking what constitutes it.
passages.rationale: Adds a powerful, poetic expression of what the base gestures toward but doesn't fully articulate—that awareness itself is the deathless, and glimpsing this is a flash into Nibbana.

passages.quote: Whatever you are doing, when it finishes, STOP! Acknowledge that that action of thought, speech or deed has come to an end and will never happen again.
passages.integration_hint: Add alongside the base's practical instruction about saying 'thank you, goodbye' internally after pleasant experiences.
passages.rationale: Offers a complementary daily-life practice instruction that is more direct and action-oriented, reinforcing the base's teaching on seeing endings with a concrete technique.
